description: Generic NVMe SSD health monitoring template. Uses the agent's PhysicalDisk.* metric family (which wraps smartctl -a -j) to collect identity, capacity, SMART pass/fail status and the universal NVMe health log fields defined by the NVMe specification: critical_warning, temperature, percentage_used (drive wear estimate), available_spare / available_spare_threshold, media_errors, unsafe_shutdowns, warning_temp_time, critical_comp_time. NVMe SMART data is structurally different from ATA SMART (it lives in nvme_smart_health_information_log as a flat object rather than in ata_smart_attributes/table), so PhysicalDisk.SmartAttrRaw / SmartAttrWorst / SmartAttrThreshold do not apply here; this template uses PhysicalDisk.SmartAttr which falls back to looking up the field name directly in the NVMe health log. Instance discovery is via the PhysicalDisk.Devices agent list, filtered to devices reported by smartctl as type 'nvme'. The agent must run with sufficient privileges to invoke smartctl on the target devices (root on Linux, Administrator on Windows). The template is not auto-bound - bind manually to nodes that have NVMe drives you want to monitor.

templates.name: NVMe SSD Health
templates.comments:
Generic NVMe SSD monitoring via the NetXMS agent's PhysicalDisk.* metrics.

Instance discovery:
  - Source: PhysicalDisk.Devices agent list (smartctl --scan).
  - Filter: only devices whose smartctl device type is 'nvme'.
  - Display name: device path followed by model in parentheses, e.g. '/dev/nvme0 (WDC PC SN730 SDBQNTY-512G-1001)'.

Metric coverage and thresholds:
  Identity (polled hourly - changes rarely):
    PhysicalDisk.Model         -> model name
    PhysicalDisk.Firmware      -> firmware version
    PhysicalDisk.SerialNumber  -> serial number
    PhysicalDisk.Capacity      -> user capacity in bytes
  Health:
    PhysicalDisk.SmartStatus              -> overall SMART pass/fail (every 5 min); transformed 'true'/'false' to 1/0. Threshold fires when value != 1.
    PhysicalDisk.SmartAttr(critical_warning) -> NVMe critical warning bitfield. Each bit indicates a different fault (bit 0=spare below threshold, 1=temp exceeded threshold, 2=reliability degraded, 3=read-only, 4=volatile memory backup failed, 5=persistent memory region degraded). Threshold fires when value != 0.
    PhysicalDisk.Temperature              -> warning >= 70 degC, critical >= 80 degC. NVMe drives run hotter than SATA; the NVMe spec allows up to 85 degC composite temperature on most consumer parts.
  Wear / lifetime:
    PhysicalDisk.SmartAttr(percentage_used) -> drive's own wear estimate (0..255, where 100 = rated endurance reached). Warning >= 80, critical >= 95. Values above 100 are valid - the drive is past its rated TBW but may still be working.
    PhysicalDisk.SmartAttr(available_spare) -> percentage of spare blocks remaining (100=fresh, 0=exhausted). Warning <= 20, critical <= the firmware-reported available_spare_threshold (mirrored as a separate DCI for visibility).
    PhysicalDisk.SmartAttr(available_spare_threshold) -> the firmware's own threshold for the available_spare warning. Static, polled hourly.
    PhysicalDisk.PowerOnTime              -> hours
    PhysicalDisk.PowerCycles              -> count
  Errors / events:
    PhysicalDisk.SmartAttr(media_errors)       -> count of unrecovered data integrity errors. Threshold fires on any non-zero value.
    PhysicalDisk.SmartAttr(unsafe_shutdowns)   -> count of power losses without a clean shutdown. Informational, no threshold (cumulative; high values across reboots are normal on hardware that does not let the OS shut the drive down cleanly).
    PhysicalDisk.SmartAttr(warning_temp_time)  -> minutes the drive has spent above the warning temperature. Threshold fires on any non-zero increase (sustained hot operation).
    PhysicalDisk.SmartAttr(critical_comp_time) -> minutes the drive has spent above the critical composite temperature. Threshold fires on any non-zero value.

Notes:
  - The agent caches smartctl output for 60 seconds per device.
  - PhysicalDisk.SmartAttr on NVMe falls back to nvme_smart_health_information_log/<name> when the argument is not a top-level JSON path. The lookup is by field name, not numeric ID, since NVMe health log fields are named (no SMART attribute IDs as in ATA).
  - PhysicalDisk.SmartAttrRaw / SmartAttrWorst / SmartAttrThreshold do not work on NVMe (they only walk ata_smart_attributes/table). Use PhysicalDisk.SmartAttr instead.
  - data_units_read / data_units_written and host_reads / host_writes are intentionally omitted - they are workload metrics, not health metrics. Add them as a separate workload template if you need them.

dcis.name: PhysicalDisk.Model({instance})
dcis.comments: Model name reported by smartctl (model_name field in JSON output). Polled hourly.

dcis.name: PhysicalDisk.Firmware({instance})
dcis.comments: Firmware version (firmware_version field in smartctl JSON output). Polled hourly.

dcis.name: PhysicalDisk.SerialNumber({instance})
dcis.comments: Serial number (serial_number field in smartctl JSON output). Polled hourly.

dcis.name: PhysicalDisk.Capacity({instance})
dcis.comments: User capacity in bytes (user_capacity/bytes in smartctl JSON output). 64-bit unsigned. Polled hourly.

dcis.name: PhysicalDisk.SmartStatus({instance})
dcis.comments:
Overall SMART pass/fail flag from the controller (smart_status/passed in smartctl JSON output). The agent returns the JSON boolean as the literal string 'true' or 'false', so the raw data type is STRING; the transformation converts to 1 (passed) / 0 (failed) and stores as INT. Polled every 5 minutes. Threshold fires on any value other than 1.

Note: on NVMe, smart_status/passed is derived from critical_warning - it is 'false' whenever any critical_warning bit is set. The critical_warning DCI gives a more precise picture; this DCI is kept for parity with the SATA template.

dcis.name: PhysicalDisk.SmartAttr({instance},critical_warning)
dcis.comments:
NVMe critical_warning byte (nvme_smart_health_information_log/critical_warning). Bitfield of fault conditions:
  bit 0 (0x01) - available spare has fallen below threshold
  bit 1 (0x02) - temperature has exceeded over- or under-temp threshold
  bit 2 (0x04) - NVM subsystem reliability degraded due to significant media-related errors
  bit 3 (0x08) - media has been placed in read-only mode
  bit 4 (0x10) - volatile memory backup device has failed
  bit 5 (0x20) - persistent memory region has become read-only or unreliable
Value 0 = healthy. Threshold fires on any non-zero value.

Looked up directly in nvme_smart_health_information_log/critical_warning by the SmartAttr fallback path.

dcis.name: PhysicalDisk.Temperature({instance})
dcis.comments: Drive composite temperature in degrees Celsius (temperature/current in smartctl JSON output, ultimately from the NVMe SMART log). NVMe drives commonly run 50-70 degC under sustained workload; thresholds set to warning >= 70, critical >= 80. The NVMe spec allows up to 85 degC composite temperature on most consumer parts; the controller will throttle and eventually shut down on its own above that. Tighten if your application requires it.

dcis.name: PhysicalDisk.SmartAttr({instance},percentage_used)
dcis.comments: Drive's own wear estimate (nvme_smart_health_information_log/percentage_used). Vendor-defined estimate of how much of the drive's rated endurance has been consumed. 0 = fresh, 100 = rated endurance reached. Per NVMe spec the value can exceed 100 - the drive does not stop working at 100, but warranty/TBW is exhausted. Warning >= 80, critical >= 95.

dcis.name: PhysicalDisk.SmartAttr({instance},available_spare)
dcis.comments: Percentage of remaining spare blocks (nvme_smart_health_information_log/available_spare). 100 = full spare pool, 0 = exhausted. The controller sets critical_warning bit 0 when this drops below available_spare_threshold (typically 10%). Warning <= 20, critical <= 10.

dcis.name: PhysicalDisk.SmartAttr({instance},available_spare_threshold)
dcis.comments: Firmware-defined threshold for the available_spare warning (nvme_smart_health_information_log/available_spare_threshold). When available_spare drops below this value the controller sets critical_warning bit 0. Static value defined by the drive vendor (typically 10%). Polled hourly. No threshold - shown for reference alongside available_spare.

dcis.name: PhysicalDisk.PowerOnTime({instance})
dcis.comments: Power-on time in hours (power_on_time/hours in smartctl JSON output, mirrored from the NVMe health log). Polled hourly.

dcis.name: PhysicalDisk.PowerCycles({instance})
dcis.comments: Number of power-on cycles (power_cycle_count in smartctl JSON output, mirrored from the NVMe health log). Polled hourly.

dcis.name: PhysicalDisk.SmartAttr({instance},media_errors)
dcis.comments: Cumulative count of unrecovered data integrity errors detected by the controller (nvme_smart_health_information_log/media_errors). Includes uncorrected ECC errors, CRC errors, and similar low-level data corruption events. A non-zero value means the drive has experienced data loss the controller could not recover. Threshold fires on any non-zero count.

dcis.name: PhysicalDisk.SmartAttr({instance},unsafe_shutdowns)
dcis.comments: Cumulative count of power losses without a clean controller shutdown notification (nvme_smart_health_information_log/unsafe_shutdowns). Polled hourly. No threshold - this is informational. High values are typical on systems where the OS does not get a chance to send the NVMe shutdown notification before power-off (kernel panic, force-off, certain BIOS power configurations) and do not by themselves indicate a drive problem.

dcis.name: PhysicalDisk.SmartAttr({instance},warning_temp_time)
dcis.comments: Cumulative minutes the controller has run above its warning composite temperature threshold (nvme_smart_health_information_log/warning_temp_time). The threshold itself is set by the firmware (vendor-specific, typically 70-75 degC). Threshold fires on any non-zero value - sustained operation above the warning temperature shortens drive life.

dcis.name: PhysicalDisk.SmartAttr({instance},critical_comp_time)
dcis.comments: Cumulative minutes the controller has run above its critical composite temperature threshold (nvme_smart_health_information_log/critical_comp_time). Above this temperature the controller will throttle aggressively or shut down to protect the media. Threshold fires on any non-zero value - any time spent above critical is a real cooling problem.
